steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: bowel obstruction, gynecologic pathology (pelvic abscess, ovarian pathology), colonic neoplasm with perforation or obstruction
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and extent of the intra-abdominal infection/inflammation causing LLQ/suprapubic pain, fever, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, markedly elevated CRP, and functional large-bowel obstruction (no BM or flatus) has not been characterized — the chest X-ray and UA add no abdominal detail, and the clinical picture is too complex for empirical management alone.
ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolic fat stranding with wall thickening of the sigmoid colon consistent with diverticulitis, with or without a pericolonic abscess or free air; alternatively, a pelvic mass or free fluid suggesting a gynecologic process or complicated colonic pathology
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has fever, markedly elevated CRP (136.6 mg/L), neutrophilia (ANC 10.41, 85.8%), LLQ/suprapubic pain with abdominal distension, no BM or flatus, and a clean UA ruling out UTI — CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the standard first-line imaging to identify and characterize the abdominal source.
grounding: History: 'lower abdominal pain...suprapubic region and worse in the left lower quadrant'
History: 'fevers to 101, chills, nausea and anorexia'
History: 'no BM or flatus since the pain started'
History: 'difficulty urinating secondary to pain'
History: 'started on oral ciprofloxacin for presumed UTI though her ua today is clean'
Laboratory tests: 'C-Reactive Protein: 136.6 mg/L (ref 0.0-5.0)'
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 12.1 K/uL', 'Neutrophils: 85.8%', 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 10.41 K/uL'
Laboratory tests: 'Sodium: 129.0 mEq/L' — hyponatremia suggesting systemic inflammatory/stress response
Physical examination: 'soft, appropriately tender, mildly distended. drain removed. drain site c/d/i'
Prior imaging: Chest X-ray — 'The lungs are clear besides mild right basilar atelectasis' — no free air under diaphragm identified, abdominal source not imaged
ensemble.reasoning: This patient presents with progressive LLQ/suprapubic pain, fever, markedly elevated CRP, neutrophilic leukocytosis, functional obstruction (no BM/flatus), and a clean UA excluding UTI — a picture strongly suggesting an acute intra-abdominal inflammatory process, most likely sigmoid diverticulitis given her age, pain location, and prior colonoscopy showing polyps, but gynecologic pathology and complicated colonic disease cannot be excluded. The chest X-ray provides no abdominal anatomic information, and the physical exam note of a 'drain removed' implies there may be an ongoing or prior abdominal process requiring reassessment. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is ordered to identify the source, characterize severity (abscess, perforation, obstruction), and guide definitive management.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: gynecologic pathology (ovarian abscess, tubo-ovarian abscess, pelvic inflammatory disease), bowel obstruction, colonic malignancy or perforation

ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding with wall thickening of the sigmoid or descending colon consistent with acute diverticulitis (possibly with abscess or perforation), OR a pelvic mass/fluid collection suggesting gynecologic origin, OR evidence of large-bowel obstruction — with contrast enhancement to assess bowel wall viability and vascular involvement
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has progressive LLQ/suprapubic pain, fever, obstipation, distension, markedly elevated CRP (136.6 mg/L), leukocytosis with neutrophilia (ANC 10.41, 85.8% neutrophils), and a clean UA that refutes UTI — a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the standard first-line cross-sectional study to identify and characterize the underlying structural pathology in this clinical scenario.

ensemble.reasoning: This patient presents with progressive LLQ and suprapubic pain, fever, obstipation, marked systemic inflammation (CRP 136.6, neutrophilic leukocytosis), and urinary retention — all pointing to a significant intraperitoneal or pelvic structural process. The clean UA effectively excludes UTI as the driver, and the only prior imaging (chest X-ray) does not characterize the abdomen. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the most efficient single test to localize the source, assess for complications such as abscess or perforation (especially given sigmoid diverticulitis as the leading hypothesis given LLQ predominance), and evaluate for gynecologic or obstructive etiologies.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated sigmoid diverticulitis with wall thickening, significant pericolic fat stranding, and a sizable air-fluid collection (abscess) extending from the retrouterine pouch posteriorly and superiorly above the sigmoid colon, measuring up to 8.1 × 2.3 × 5.5 cm.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly confirmed the expected finding of sigmoid diverticulitis with pericolic stranding and wall thickening, and additionally identified a pericolonic/pelvic abscess, which was explicitly listed as an alternative expected outcome.

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: The patient has known complicated sigmoid diverticulitis with multi-compartment abscess collections that were only partially drained on the prior CT, plus a new intramural abscess component identified at that time. The drain has since been removed, the ultrasound was explicitly inadequate to assess the collections, and the patient still has markedly elevated CRP (136.6 mg/L) and leukocytosis (WBC 12.1, ANC 10.41) suggesting ongoing or incompletely treated infection. Repeat contrast CT is the only modality that can reliably assess whether the abscesses have resolved, persisted, or progressed — information essential to deciding between continued conservative management versus surgical intervention.

vindication.actual_finding: The posterior abscess decreased in size (4.1 x 1.7 cm from 4.9 x 1.7 cm) with interval resolution of its fluid component, the intramural sigmoid abscess decreased in size with only residual gas, and the retrouterine collection was grossly stable at 3.1 x 2.2 cm with no new or enlarging abscesses.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly addressed all three collections the physician was tracking, showing partial response to drainage for the superior collection, reduction of the intramural abscess (rather than progression), and stability of the retrouterine collection, consistent with the expected assessment of interval change and drain efficacy.
